items.title: Touch of Life
items.summary: Chapter 1 Orion woke and immediately touched his forehead. Huh, I’m alive... wait, I’m standing? Looking around, he realized he was in the middle of a narrow street. It was a dark night, with a dim, yellowish streetlight in the distance casting blurry shadows on…
items.content_html:
Chapter 1

Orion woke and immediately touched his forehead. Huh, I’m alive... wait, I’m standing? Looking around, he realized he was in the middle of a narrow street. It was a dark night, with a dim, yellowish streetlight in the distance casting blurry shadows on the trees lining the road. The air felt somehow cold, as it had just rained. The road was still wet, and water was dripping from the leaves.

How did I get here? It took him a while to concentrate. Wait, I remember someone pointing a gun at me; then something hit my forehead like I’d been kicked by a horse… He touched his forehead again: it was smooth and dry, with no hole or blood. Who was it? It had felt like a bullet drilled into my head… He felt his arms, hands, legs, and toes. Everything was fine.  

This unpaved street was in the middle of nowhere; no houses or buildings were in sight, not even a stop sign or speed limit. Not knowing what else to do, he started walking. The sound of his footsteps on the dirt and small rocks scratched into the eerie silence.

A car approached from behind, but he didn’t look. When the car slowly passed him, his peripheral vision caught a glimpse of a young kid in the passenger seat looking at him funny.

He walked for about half an hour before seeing some dingy wooden houses on the left. They were old and run-down, with broken doorsteps and cracked windows. A bit further, some people were lounging, smoking, or talking around a fire. They were all in thick coats. They became quiet when he walked near them, staring at him as though seeing a ghost.

He didn’t know why, but he felt compelled to keep walking.

There were more run-down houses and ragged people. A group of three glared in his direction, then the one with a beard coughed. They walked up to block his way. “Hey bro, what’s up?” the bearded one said.

“Not much,” Orion replied. A hint of foul smell mixing tobacco, cheap booze, and other nameless sour odors like sweat dried in the sun found its way to his nose.

“You ain’t cold? What are you doing out so late around here?”

“I don’t know.”

They looked at each other and smirked. “Alright, you got some cash? My bro here needs a few dimes for his child support, you know what it’s like.”

Orion knew this was a robbery, but a strange calmness lodged in his chest, or maybe a lack of emotions. He searched his pocket, fished out his wallet, and opened it. “Sorry, no cash.”

“Plastic will do, too,” the bearded man laughed.

“But that’s mine.”

“You sure about that?” Another guy with a tattoo of a dragon on his right forearm pulled out a big knife.

Orion shrugged.

“Oh,” they acted surprised, looked at each other and laughed. The third guy, who had two nose rings, said, “So you wanna play it the hard way, huh? Tough guy?”

People started gathering. “Give them what they want, it’s not worth it,” some guy shouted.

“Listen to them, bro,” the bearded man said.

Orion put his wallet back into his pocket.

The tattooed guy pointed that big knife right at Orion’s face, only two inches away from his nose. “Give.”

Why isn’t my heart pounding? Shouldn’t I be scared? Orion replied, “We don’t have to do this… how about I buy you drinks? Dinner?”   

“Shut the fuck up and give me the fucking wallet,” the tattooed guy said in a low, threatening voice. “Another word and you’ll be crying for your mommy.”

“Listen…”

The knife slashed through Orion’s right arm, from bicep to wrist. Some among the crowd screamed. Then absolute silence.

“How…” The tattooed guy’s eyes grew big. Orion looked at his right arm, then looked up at the three guys; his face turned a little paler with more puzzlement.

“You didn’t cut him?” the nose ring guy asked.

“I did, I did, I…” The tattooed guy was shaking a little, then yelled, “Fuck!” and slashed across Orion’s right arm and his abdomen. It should have cut his stomach open.

The shirt tore, but his belly was untouched.

“Fuck! What’s this shit? Fuck you!” the tattooed guy shrieked at the top of his lungs, “I’m gonna kill you! You creep! Die now! Die!” he stabbed Orion in the chest five times, ten times, fifteen times… After he had stopped stabbing, Orion just stood there looking at him. The tattooed guy was panting and trembling.

“This is fucking creepy…” a voice came from the crowd. “Fuck this shit, I’m out of here!” Suddenly everyone started screaming and scattered. Orion was alone with his long ghostly shadow.

The next morning at around seven.

The sun's rays began to break through the clouds, painting the sky orange and yellow. A small restaurant, Green Creek, had a few guests enjoying their scrambled eggs and coffee. A young waitress was taking orders. The smell of freshly fried bacon and waffles wafted nearly two blocks away, drawing a few stray dogs and cats to linger nearby.

A bald middle-aged man with a mustache was sipping his coffee when he saw a miserable-looking man open the door. It was Orion. This was mid-December, but he only wore a torn navy blue short-sleeve shirt that barely held together, and a pair of jeans. Some frost formed on the tips of his hair. He didn’t seem cold though. The bald man stopped sipping.

Orion found a seat near him and sat down. Everyone in the restaurant couldn’t help but stare. The waitress very slowly walked up to Orion’s table.

“Can I have a cup of coffee and a shepherd’s pie?” Orion said.

“Sure… you okay, sir? Do you need anything? A blanket maybe?” the waitress asked.

“No, I’m fine; thank you for asking,” he grinned. Maybe the grin was to be reassuring, but everyone felt their hair standing up.

She poured a cup of coffee for him and slowly walked away, still looking at him.

A few more guests came in. The noise of people chatting, the busboy collecting plates and utensils, and the occasional clanking from the kitchen grew louder and more expansive, yet Orion’s attention stayed on his exposed torso, eyes drifting downward as he continued to feel it.

“Hey, sir,” the bald man said. “Wanna sit with me?”

Orion kept on touching his own chest, seeming distracted and disturbed.

“Sir?”

Orion raised his head, “Yes, sorry?”

“You want to sit here?” the bald man pointed at the seat across from him.   

(To be continued)

items.title: Sexual Infidelity: What Are the Predictors, According to Research?
items.summary: What kinds of people/situations are more likely to lead to affairs? When I was a young teen, I already had a clear idea of what I’d do if my future spouse cheated on me: divorce. No other alternative. However, as I grew older, had my…
items.content_html:
What kinds of people/situations are more likely to lead to affairs?

When I was a young teen, I already had a clear idea of what I’d do if my future spouse cheated on me: divorce. No other alternative. However, as I grew older, had my own family, have seen more, I gradually realized the issue was way more complicated than I’d believed.

There are, of course, tons of studies on this topic. What I’m going to do here is just review some quick summaries, and I’ll only focus on the most “stereotypical” type: sexual infidelity.

But first, how prevalent is it? It’s not that easy to find out the exact number because few people will be willing to openly admit it. In general, research suggests that it’s around 20 – 25% in America (for example, see here and here); in other words, one in about four to five people will have sex with someone other than their spouse/partner at least once. Dr. Mark at Indiana University and her colleagues stated that infidelity is the most common reason for divorce in America, and Dr. Betzig at the University of Michigan also claimed that it’s the #1 reason in at least 160 other countries. Guess too many people think with that thing between their legs and their spouses aren’t happy about it.

Dr. Atkins, a clinical psychologist at the University of Washington, and his colleagues analyzed a big data set from national surveys of around 4,000 people. Let’s break down the findings into different categories.

Demographic factors

Men vs. Women  

Yes, according to this study, men are more likely to have sexual affairs than women. But what’s more interesting is the probability changes throughout different ages: men between the ages of 55 and 65 at the time of the survey are the most likely to report infidelity (about 15%). Is it because men who reach these ages typically have the itch? Or because of the “cohort effect,” which means these male respondents grew up in a certain culture that somehow “encourages” infidelity? We don’t really know, but the researchers infer it’s because of the cohort effect.

Women have a much lower probability (around 4 – 5%), and they’re more likely to report infidelity between the ages of 40 and 45. Again, this might NOT be saying women of these ages want to have affairs; it might be the effect of the culture experienced in this particular age group, we don’t really know.

However, this study as well as Dr. Fincham at Florida State University and his colleague also pointed out that this gap between men and women is closing. Women nowadays represent a significant part of the workforce, which means they have more financial power, independence, and opportunity for infidelity.  

Education

Respondents with graduate degrees AND who have previously divorced are 1.75 times more likely to have affairs (again) than those with less than high school diplomas. The study didn’t say much about possible reasons, my guess is that these people are more likely to have status and opportunity.

Religion

People who report attending church regularly AND are highly satisfied with their marriages (reporting “very happy”) have a lower likelihood of infidelity. For those who attend church regularly but report marriage satisfaction as “pretty happy” or “not too happy,” their religious behavior isn’t associated with any lower probability of infidelity.

Looking at what other studies find, we know that religion is a complicated factor. The study in 2007 must be loved by many faithful church-goers: they found that people who see the Bible as the literal word of God are significantly less likely to have affairs. On the other hand, however, this study by Iranian researchers in 2019 and Dr. Mark’s research found no such relationship.

I think maybe one reason for the big discrepancy is that people attend church for a variety of motives. Some truly internalize the values of religious teaching, while others go to the chapel just because that’s another place to talk and maybe eat, but the surveys might have a harder time catching that. Just my speculation though.

History of divorce

Yup, people who’ve divorced before are nearly twice as likely to have affairs again than those who’ve never divorced.

Incomes/employment status

The study put incomes and employment status together as an indicator of opportunity. For incomes, among people who make $30,000/year or more (about $45,755 in 2021, see this calculator), the more they make the more likelihood of having affairs. No such relationship among people making less than $30,000. It’s suggested that with higher incomes, people might have higher status and more opportunities for traveling or seeing new people. Also, they didn’t ask the incomes of more than $75,000/year (about $114,386 in 2021); they thought that maybe for crazy-rich people the relationship would be even stronger.  

For employment status, they have four categories: respondents and their spouses both are employed, respondents and their spouses both are unemployed, respondents are employed but their spouses aren’t, and respondents aren’t employed but their spouses are. The “respondents are employed but their spouses aren’t” category is the most likely to have affairs—probably because this signifies the most power difference. The “both are unemployed” is the least likely to have affairs, guess they don’t have the time, energy, or chance.

Age of first marriage

The younger the people got married the first time, the more likely they’ll have affairs. Those who were first married at 16 (goodness) are almost 4 times more likely to have affairs than people at 23. This is expected: if you ask me, teens are, in general, impulsive, immature, and not experienced. When I was a teen I was stupid like hell, can’t imagine what would have happened if I were married at 16.

Relationship

Relationship satisfaction

People might think only those who are in unhappy marriages will go out and seek alternatives, but the researchers of this study reviewed the literature and found that some studies indicated no such relationship. Some, however, did suggest that inadequate marital qualities, e.g., low relationship satisfaction and low frequency of sex were quite significant predictors of infidelity. One of the purposes of this study was to find out which side is supported in the population they studied. And they found it: the data analyses indicated that compared to those who report “very happy” in their relationship, people who report “pretty happy” are almost 2 times more likely to have affairs, and people who report “not too happy” are 4 times more likely. Further, researchers at Pennsylvania State University suggested that low marital satisfaction can both be the cause and the consequence of infidelity.  

Note: this is by no means an excuse for affairs (“I have an affair because I’m not happy in my relationship!”); for one thing, this unhappy relationship might be your fault. It’s rarely only one partner’s fault anyway.

Commitment

In terms of relationship satisfaction, Dr. Drigotas, a psychologist at Johns Hopkins University, along with his colleagues stated that commitment is one crucial key to higher satisfaction, which predicts a lower probability of infidelity. People with a stronger commitment have higher attachment and desire to continue the relationships.

Further, people who cohabit before marriage are more likely to have affairs (for example, see this study). I think “commitment” might explain why: the idea of cohabitation is “let’s try and see if this works,” which implies a lack of commitment.  

Therefore, it seems that for a relationship to work well, both have to be committed.

Besides these factors above reviewed by Dr. Atkins and his colleagues, Dr. Fincham and his colleagues also summarized some other factors. 

Similarities

According to research, “opposites attract” doesn’t work in long-term relationships. Interestingly, according to Drs. Brooks and Monaco, the economists at the University of Wisconsin at LaCrosse, couples who are devoted to the same religion and have higher levels of education have lower probabilities of infidelity. In general, these two factors are people’s core values, and it’s hard to stay with someone with different core values.

Personality/individual

Neuroticism

People who are neurotic (emotionally unstable, prone to negative emotions such as anger, anxiety, depression; for example, see this study) are more likely to have affairs. As you can see, neurotic individuals are not happy people.  

Attachment styles

An insecure attachment style is associated with higher chances of infidelity, but this study doesn’t specify which style. Luckily, psychologists at Florida State University found that those higher in anxiety attachment are more likely to have affairs.  

Attachment styles are too huge a topic to explain here, I’ll go into details in another article. Basically, anxious style individuals are always highly on guard for the relationship, constantly worry that they’re not good enough and therefore will be abandoned; they’re those who are clingy to their partners. Strange and counter-intuitive, right? If you’re afraid to be abandoned, why would you go find someone else? We’ll talk about it in another piece.

Behavioral problems

Drug/alcohol abuse is positively related to infidelity; the more use of drugs, the higher chance of infidelity.

Attitude toward sex

Yup, of course: those who can “separate sex from love,” those who see casual sex as no big deal are more likely to f*k someone else.

Family of origin

An individual’s parent(s) have an impact too. Those who see parental infidelity are twice more likely to do it themselves than those who don’t have parental infidelity. It can be “monkey see, monkey do,” but the reasons might be way more complicated than that.

Internet

Oh yeah, the internet is another whole new world, isn’t it? This study said that 20 – 33% of people use the internet for sex, and among these people, 65% do it. To make it worse, more than half of them engage in unprotected sex. This has become a problem of spreading STDs and HIV, and therefore a costly issue for public health.

POTENTIAL APPLICATION

Okay, at this point, some of you might be getting nervous: oh no, I/my partner got many of these factors, are we doomed?

Dr. Atkins advised not to panic because of the nature of the research: this study, along with all related investigations, is correlational. In other words, they only examine relationships, not cause-and-effect. If we can gather a bunch of married people in a huge lab, randomly assign them to the “opportunity” group with many other potential sexual partners, and the “no opportunity” group with no potential sexual partners (maybe very old people?), then see what will happen in maybe three years, then we might establish cause-and-effect on whether the opportunity factor is indeed a cause. That sounds like fun, but we can’t do that.

Correlation is not causation, so we have to be careful when we interpret these findings.

However, they still offered some potential suggestions. First, because the combination of one working and one staying home is the most vulnerable type to infidelity,

…couples with one working partner might give particular attention to how to maintain a sense of equality in power and control in the marriage and develop strategies to interact with other individuals and couples together.

They also recommended couples keep working on their relationships to achieve high standards of “very happy” and high satisfaction.   

And finally, these are my takeaways. From what I’ve seen, infidelity is complicated, and sometimes it’s not necessarily 100% the fault of the one who commits adultery, but the consequence is damaging. In many cases, it doesn’t only end in divorce but much farther and longer. In general, to prevent heartbreaks, commitment is the key, as Dr. Digotas’s research indicated. It can be a little like choosing a career: you don’t jump around whenever you see problems.  

And to be able to commit, a shared, common goal or goals is a vital component. I read somewhere about this: “Love is not only in couple looking at each other, but also in them looking toward the same direction” or words to that effect.

On the other hand, however, some infidelity is from much deeper and troublesome underlying problems, e.g., addiction, mental health issues, or even abuse. They have to be addressed before any reconciliation can happen, and maybe these people need a little more empathetic eye.

items.title: To Combat Inflammation in My Body, I Eat Two Superfoods and Avoid Three
items.summary: Chronic inflammation is associated with diseases such as cancer, diabetes, heart disease Inflammation in our body is a normal, healthy response; it becomes a problem only when it is a long-term condition due to particular habits. According to Harvard Health Publishing (2019, 2020), inflammation is…
items.content_html:
Chronic inflammation is associated with diseases such as cancer, diabetes, heart disease

Inflammation in our body is a normal, healthy response; it becomes a problem only when it is a long-term condition due to particular habits. According to Harvard Health Publishing (2019, 2020), inflammation is when our immune system fights off foreign invaders—but certain lifestyle habits (e.g., smoking or constant consumption of foods high in sugar) can evoke lingering immune responses that lead to serious illnesses like heart disease or diabetes, and one of the best ways to prevent or treat the condition is to eat foods with anti-inflammatory effects.

Since I was a kid, adults would tell me not to eat too much of certain foods because they caused “fire” in our bodies to rise. This idea of “fire” within us, of course, is from Chinese medicine (e.g., Thomson Chinese Medicine, n.d.). Based on my quick research, I have a strong feeling that this “fire” idea is referring to inflammation; at least they should be related. For one thing, many websites in Mandarin (e.g., here, in case you use Google Translate) explain that chronic “fire” can lead to heart disease and diabetes, same with inflammation.

The symptoms of too much fire, according to the Mandarin sites and what I’ve learned growing up, include swelling of the mouth and gums, acne flaring up, bad breath, thirst, maybe constipation, and headaches, to name a few. Among them, the first two are the most bothersome to me.

Whenever I don’t sleep well, I know I have a bunch of troubles waiting for me the next day. I think everyone is different and therefore will have different symptoms, but for me, I’ll find myself way more likely to bite my lip or tongue—the sign that they’re swelling. Then either my nose, lip, face, head, or even annoying private areas like my butt will start feeling pain; when I feel the aching spot, yup, a huge lump is forming under the skin waiting to become a zit like a volcano. It’s not only annoying and inconvenient but also painful like hell.

Many sources (e.g., Harvard Health Publishing, 2020) list anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens, fruits, legumes, fish with Omega-3 fatty acids (e.g., salmon, tuna), nuts. From the wisdom of the adults around me in my childhood, and also from my personal experience, I find two foods to be the most effective: grapefruit and mung beans.  

Two of my superfoods

1. Grapefruit

Some might not like the slightly bitter taste, and if you choose one that happens to be sour it’s quite unpleasant. Plus, eating it isn’t the most convenient thing. But grapefruit is high in nutrients and low in calories, and when it’s ripe and sweet, it’s very delicious. According to Ware (2019), grapefruit is a good source of vitamins A and C, can reduce risks of diabetes, stroke, hypertension, even certain cancers, etc. It can also help with weight loss and skin health.  

If you decide to try it, I strongly recommend eating the whole fruit instead of buying juice. For one thing, without the benefit of fiber, one might consume too much sugar—even with 100% juice, there’s a lot of natural sugar in it, and sugar is sugar, too much isn’t good for you. Plus, I don’t know if grapefruit juice only will have the same anti-inflammatory effect.  

There’s another potentially serious side effect we have to be careful of: grapefruit has some special qualities that make it easy to interact with certain medications (U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), n.d.). If you’re taking medicine(s), make sure you consult a doctor first. Further, all citrus fruits can hurt teeth; if you have sensitive teeth, you’ll have to eat in moderation and rinse your mouth with water afterward (Feintuch, 2016).

If I toss and turn all night, I’ll make sure I eat one grapefruit the next day; that usually can prevent most swelling and acne problems. If necessary, I might eat another the following day. Eating a grapefruit a day for several days should be a healthy habit anyway.

2. Mung beans

Many Americans probably aren’t familiar with mung beans. They are of the legume family, small with deep green color (see photo), very common in Asia; the majority of Asian stores have them. They’re available on Amazon too, but some seem to be overpriced. Basically, if it’s more than 50 cents per oz I don’t consider it.  

Mung beans are packed with nutrients. Hou et al. (2019) stated that they’re a great source of protein, as well as fiber and antioxidants that can prevent diseases. Pahwa et al. (2020) pointed out that mung beans have anti-inflammatory effects. Anderson and Li (2021) claimed that mung bean protein can improve muscle strength. Therefore, mung beans are not only good for their anti-inflammatory effect, but also for building muscle, especially when we consider the protein is plant-based so we don’t have to worry about saturated fat.

In Taiwan, the most common recipe is sweet mung bean soup. It’s simple and straightforward: some say you better soak them in water before cooking, but I simply put about a cup of beans with three – four cups of water in a small pot, slow heat for about forty – sixty minutes or until the beans break and soften. Add some more water when needed. When they’re done, add probably two teaspoons of sugar and maybe some lightly sweetened soymilk or milk. For me, the soup is a good snack between meals, and I usually finish it in about two – four days.

If you want to try other recipes, YouTube “mung beans recipe” and you’ll get a bunch of hits. But I’ll shy away from any recipes that involve too much oil and/or sugar (e.g., fried bean pancakes) when I have “too much fire” in me. They can be delicious, but for anti-inflammation, probably not that good. Bakeries in Taiwan will also make sweets like mung bean cakes, but usually there’s a lot of sugar in it.

I read somewhere saying that mung beans taste a little like potatoes, though to me they’re not that alike. I think the main reason is I eat mung beans with some added sugar or honey, but I can’t imagine potatoes with sugar.  

Avoid three types of foods

But merely eating the two superfoods is not enough if we want to be totally free from inflammation, or the fire in the body. Besides a good night's sleep, we have to avoid certain types of foods at least temporarily. According to Harvard Health Publishing (2020), red meat, sugary foods, processed foods, deep-fried foods, pastries, and saturated fat like shortening and lard will cause inflammation.  

From the listed references, some Chinese websites (e.g., here and here), and my personal experience, here I summarize the three worst types of foods that lead to the fire.

1. Fried or greasy foods

For example: fries, burgers, fried chicken, pizza, sausage, TV dinner, even flavored crackers like cheese crackers, etc. In fact, if you see any grease on it, if it’s been in hot oil, try to avoid it in any case for now; they are probably the biggest contributor to inflammation. Yes, I guess I pretty much eliminate the majority of foods one can conveniently buy in stores. That’s why preparing your own meals is a critical part of a healthy lifestyle. For instance, instead of fried chicken, try to steam or boil the chicken. I love boiled chicken: I can use the broth for cooking a bowl of delicious noodles.  

2. Sugary foods

For example: soda, pastries, flavored drinks, candy/chocolate, flavored yogurt, granola, etc. Because we try so much to avoid fat, many “healthy” food products labeled “low fat” are loaded with sugar for taste. One just has to know how to read labels to avoid these culprits.

3. Spicy foods

Interestingly, in Chinese medicine, spicy foods aren’t the best choices. In fact, Chinese medicine claims that any food can be unhealthy if it’s heavily spiced. However, this view is a huge contrast compared to Western medicine. For example, the University of Pennsylvania Medicine (2019) listed all the benefits of spicy foods, including “keep your heart healthy” and “promote weight loss.” 

However, a few things are for sure: first, Dr. McDonald (2018) at the University of Chicago Medicine stated that though spicy food can be healthy, if one has low tolerance for spicy flavor, eating spicy food can be too much for their stomach. Further, spicy food may irritate anal fissures. He also warned that if the spice is extremely hot (e.g., ghost peppers), it can cause serious stomach pain and vomiting. Personally, if I eat spicy foods, I’m more likely to have an acne problem.  

More importantly, many spicy foods are also loaded with salt as well. Just the other day I was in a Poki Poki restaurant in Albuquerque. Oh my goodness, the spicy taste was okay, but the whole bowl was swimming in soy sauce, it was like drinking seawater! I guess it was a bad day for that particular restaurant. Now, I believe I don’t have to cite any reference about the danger of too much sodium. I trust you might have this experience: after a meal in a restaurant you become especially thirsty. That’s because pretty much ALL restaurants add way, way too much of all the spices for taste, the high sodium levels make you desire extra glasses of water. This is simply not healthy to say the least.

Habits to stop

Food is not the only thing that causes inflammation (e.g., Zhang et al., 2015). If you smoke and/or drink alcohol, it’s time to rethink these habits. We don’t need to talk about smoking, we all know it’s bad and it kills a lot of people every day. For alcohol, even a moderate amount of drinking is not risk-free (e.g., Mayo Clinic, 2019). If you have to drink, be sure to always monitor the amount.

The takeaway

Chronic inflammation is mostly from bad habits, and it’s associated with life-threatening diseases. Eating healthy is one of the best ways to tackle the problem. Personally, I especially LOVE grapefruit (if choose right—again, the sour ones are just too much) and mung beans; they are luscious and super healthy. They do wonders for me, I hope they can have positive effects on your health as well.

items.title: Asking for a Recommendation Letter: Twelve Do’s and Don’ts From a Professor’s View
items.summary: Some are “common sense,” but there are other critical components you might not know [Chu News — Mark Chu / Education] I think it’s fair to say that in this modern society, the majority of people have to ask for a recommendation letter at some point…
items.content_html:
Some are “common sense,” but there are other critical components you might not know

[Chu News — Mark Chu / Education] I think it’s fair to say that in this modern society, the majority of people have to ask for a recommendation letter at some point in their lives. After all, the majority of higher education institutions and jobs require it; in many cases more than one. Don’t think this is just a formality: recommendation letters can be critical for a job, college, or graduate program applications. However, since I started my teaching career, I’ve been surprised by the fact that many people have no clue how to ask for one. Quite a few students thought they could ask me just because of some random reasons; some didn’t even bother to ask.

One thing I have to emphasize is that the preparation for asking for a letter starts WAY EARLIER than you think. If you’re a student, this process begins when you first step on the campus soil. If you never think about it until you need to apply for a job or graduate school, chances are it’s too late. Building enough credit for a good letter is a long process. But if you’re in that position, there might still be something you can do.

I write this with the assumption that you’re either a high school or college student. However, if you’re not, I believe the same principles apply.

So first, these are things to keep in mind when you start taking classes.

1. Your grade in the class

At least in my case, even getting an A will NOT automatically earn you a letter; there’re many other factors I have to consider. If you get a C or lower in one particular class, you probably don’t want to ask that teacher.

2. Interaction

The truth is, once again: getting a good letter requires preparation—long-term.

Let’s say you just start taking my class(es).  You need to help me know you, so the least you can do—when you’re in my class(es), NOT after—is being responsive. Better yet, take initiative and ask questions. I’m not asking you to be a teacher’s pet, but you need to show enough enthusiasm or curiosity to let me notice you. Keep in mind: even if you got an A, if you think back and realize you never answered any question/message I ask, chances are I will not know you well enough to write a good letter. You’re NOT the only A student. Especially when the class is big, or it’s an online class, you should always assume the instructor doesn’t know you unless you make an effort.

Also, do your best NOT to annoy or disappoint ANY teacher. Do not think “oh, don’t worry, I won’t ask this person as my reference.” From my experience, teachers/professors talk about students among themselves, sometimes even across different majors. Once our program was discussing which student should receive a certain award, one faculty member mentioned a name, and another shook his head, “She didn’t behave well in my class.” Other faculty didn’t have a strong opinion. Lo and behold, that award was given to someone else.

On the other hand, however, we all know one or two “psycho” teachers. If you have that, no need to be paranoid. If you show your effort and sincerity in classes, people will see that and respect you, even a psycho professor can’t deny it (try to avoid her/him still). Usually, if a teacher is psycho, other teachers know that too. I indeed saw a case of a faculty member trying to pin down a student, but other faculty disagreed and prevented further harm. If you’re in a tough position, find a teacher you can trust to help you.

3. Your effort and willingness to learn

Again, getting an A doesn’t necessarily show teachers you’re willing to put the effort into the class. Some just do the minimum required in assignments. If you don’t show enough effort/motivation, the best you’ll get is a lukewarm letter.

Student A was getting good grades in one of my classes, but toward the middle of the semester he had some different ideas about his research project. I explained to him why he shouldn’t go for these ideas, but he had the guts to use another professor’s words against mine (a new faculty member who didn’t have the related experience). I constantly clarified they were not effective, but he wouldn’t listen. It didn’t affect his grade, he still got an A. Then he decided that the new faculty was a better researcher and chose to work with her on his extended project. Sure, no problem. Unfortunately, time proved I was right and his project fell apart, along with his trust in that faculty. A while later he came to me for a letter, but how could I recommend someone who wouldn’t listen to my teaching? Not to mention he was applying to the program I went to, which taught me everything I knew? See, I do NOT mind students disagreeing with me—as long as they can offer better, valid evidence, I’ll be more than happy to receive it. But not the “I just feel like you’re wrong and I want to do it my way despite your evidence” type of disagreement.

Now, you need a letter and you’re planning to ask someone…

4. Make sure you choose the right person

This might sound like common sense, but you’ll be surprised how many people don’t think this through before they ask.

Do NOT pick a person as your reference just because s/he is a teacher/professor/boss, is nice, or is a “friend” of yours. Instead, pick the reference you have taken at least one class from or have worked with for at least 4 – 6 months, AND is impressed by your work. If you have worked with a professor on a one-on-one basis (e.g., teaching assistant, research project, club officer), that will be the best reference you can get assuming you have done a good job to impress the professor. Hint: apply for a teaching or research assistant position, even if your future goal doesn’t necessarily involve teaching/research; or a club officer, and show the professor your enthusiasm and sense of responsibility.

I had a few students who didn’t do well in my class and seemed not to care. A year later when they were applying for graduate programs, they asked for a letter. I told them that their grades didn’t look good, and though undoubtedly they were smart, I didn’t see the effort. In fact, I was a bit surprised they would ask me, I guess either they chose the wrong person, or they realized they had limited choices. Sorry, too late.

Ask yourself: how was my performance in this reference’s class(es)/job? Was it impressive enough? What will this reference say about me?

5. ASK FIRST—politely and nicely

At the very least, ask the reference, “Dear Ms/Mr./Dr./Professor XX, I’m applying ____ (details, not just a link). Can you write a rec letter for me? I’ll appreciate it very much.” beforehand. That is, do NOT give the school or job the reference’s name BEFORE you ask her/him. Quite a few times I got an email out of nowhere saying “Ms./Mr. so-and-so lists you as a reference, please evaluate this applicant’s person/writing skills…” Big mistake. First, the reference probably has taught many students; no matter how famous you are, s/he might not remember/recognize you. Second, s/he might not have time. Third, you didn’t give them a choice, and nobody likes to be forced to do something, so the letter won’t be too flattering—and trust me, you do NOT want that at all. Finally, s/he might not like you or might have nothing nice to say about you in the beginning.

6. Waive the right

I don’t know about job applications, but for higher education, especially graduate schools, they usually will ask the applicant whether s/he wants to waive the right to read the recommendation letter. I strongly recommend that you choose the “waive the right” option. If you choose not to waive the right, they’ll smell something fishy, and thus won’t take the letter seriously. Yes, you can choose not to waive the right, but then the admission committee will conclude: “The evaluation may be less candid because the writer knew that the student would see it. As a result, less weight may be assigned to such letters.” Believe me, that IS BAD.

And it’s in your best interest NOT to ask any of your references to read the letter. If you’re that worried, you probably shouldn’t have asked for this reference in the first place. In my case, I’m always honest; if I have some reservations about this student, I’ll tell her/him: “You have so and so strengths, but I’m also not so sure about X and Y… if you really want me to write one, I’ll be honest and write everything. Do you still want me to write one for you?” If your potential reference is open to it, discuss with her/him your strengths and weaknesses, then you can decide if this is a good reference for you.

7. Give the reference enough time

Trust me, you don't want the reference to rush through your recommendation. You should give your reference the specific due date, and it better be at least two weeks away. A recommendation letter isn’t like writing a note to your roommate. If you wait till the last moment, you won’t like the thing s/he writes about you.

Of course, if it’s an emergency, the reference should understand. But you’ll have to explain the situation and respect her/his decision whether or not to write a letter for you.

8. Help your reference to know your strengths

No need to be lengthy (a few short paragraphs will do), but you must give the reference an introduction of your background, e.g., your strengths, high GPA, unique work experience, leadership or service experience, etc. Give him/her your resume if proper. Also, you should refresh the reference’s memory by reminding her/him what class(es) you’ve taken from her/him (including the title(s) and when), some of the good work you’ve done in her/his class(es)/the job, even what assignment(s) or project(s) you enjoyed/learned the most. By doing so, you help the reference to understand you better, how the class(es)/job has an impact on you and feel happy (which is important if you want the reference to write good things about you). Don’t lie though—it’ll kill you. The key: show your reference that you’re a seriously persistent student/worker and are reliable.

9. Give the reference the program/job brief overview

What is the program/job about? What are the requirements? What’s the address/email? When is it due? Who should the letter be addressed to? If the reference has to find details like these by themselves, you’re annoying her/him, and that’s really not good for you. I’ll suggest you explain the program/job in your own words (again, very briefly, don’t write a long essay), do not simply give the reference a website link like saying “see for yourself.”

Nowadays, most, if not all, programs/jobs receive letters through email or an online site instead of snail mail. But in case the program/job you’re applying for does, don't ask your reference to address it and put postage on it for you. If the letter has to be mailed (instead of email), the least you can do is tell the reference that you’ll be glad to provide an envelope with postage. Even if they don’t need it, they’ll appreciate your consideration. The key: you want to be the LEAST amount of trouble, so the reference is not annoyed by having to do work you could have done for them (and should have).

10. But don’t be shy if you need more than one letter

I can’t emphasize this enough: our job as a teacher is to HELP YOU SUCCEED—as long as you’ve done your part. Usually, a student will apply to more than one program or job, and we totally understand that. When I looked for a college professor job, I applied to sixty schools. Some might be afraid to bother a reference with so many letters. Don’t be. In the majority of cases, all they need to do is copy and paste.

11. Follow up

When the due date for the program or job is approaching (like several days away), you should first ask the program or job whether they’ve received the letter. If they say no, you must follow up with the reference. Ask nicely. For example: “Dear Ms./Mr./Dr./Professor XX, I appreciate your time for writing the letter for me. I checked with the program/job, and they said they haven’t received your letter. Just wondering if you have mailed it. Thanks for your help.” The reference is obligated to reply promptly and act quickly if s/he hasn’t sent the letter.

In very rare cases (but I’ve met at least one), some shitty references will NEVER send the letter and even have the guts to LIE to you that s/he has sent it. If you want to prevent this from causing irreversible damage, maybe at least prepare another name in case of emergency. When it’s obvious this fuck-up reference decides to be a jerk, contact the name right away.

12. A thank-you note will be nice

After all the things are done, the letter is sent and the program/job has given you the decision, it will be nice if you send the reference a quick thank-you note with the update. We would like to keep records, and we care about you. Plus, you might need this person’s reference again in the future.

Takeaway

A recommendation letter can be the factor that makes a difference. Starting from now, show your potential reference(s) your effort and reliability; at least to me they are what matter the most. Build good, professional relationships. They will be good resources in the future.

items.title: How We Got Paid to Get Our PhD's While Raising Three Kids
items.summary: (and you can too) [Chu News — Mark Chu / Education] First, let me briefly tell you about my personal journey to my PhD. Hopefully, it gives you an idea about what it takes, and what you might be able to do if you want…
items.content_html:
(and you can too)

[Chu News — Mark Chu / Education] First, let me briefly tell you about my personal journey to my PhD. Hopefully, it gives you an idea about what it takes, and what you might be able to do if you want a PhD. with a limited budget too.

When I arrived in Hawaii for college, I had a small bank account and barely knew enough English to communicate. Before coming to America, one of my best friends questioned me on the phone, “Do you have enough money? Do you have enough language skills?”

“No, no.”

“That’s stupid. I predict, no, guarantee, you’ll come back in a year, top.”

“Well, I want to try,” I argued. 

“Yeah, good luck. You’ll need it. A lot.”

Four and a half years later, I graduated with honors and brought home a wife and two little babies. This crazy college business had a surprisingly good ending, and everyone thought that should be the end of it.

However, after staying in a job I hated for one plus year — I started fantasizing about smashing into the car in front of me— and also due to the nagging thought of “what if we could have a Master’s?” We both again applied to a small Master’s program in counseling in Kansas. On top of that, my wife gave birth to our third child.

Again, our decision sparked many pieces of advice like fire shooting at us: “Are you crazy!? You want to go back to America again with 3 little kids??” “And both of you going to school full time?!” “How much money do you have? What? $2,000!? I didn’t know your kids feed on dirt.”

“The school offers both of us Teaching Assistantships, which means we don’t have to pay a dime for tuition, and we get paid,” I said. Till today, I’m still surprised that so many students have no clue about this. But more about that later.

“That’s amazing! How did you know about this opportunity?” at least one asked, then added, “But you guys are still crazy. Who will take care of your kids when you both are going to school or working?”

“We’ll manage. You just watch,” I raised my head high.

Well, this time, we learned our lesson.

Because we were so damn poor, all we could afford was a run-down apartment. Whenever I put a pan on the stove, some roaches the size of a nickel would drop into it. Pretty soon they were everywhere, crawling on the floor and tables teasing us “you can’t catch me.” A month later, every single one of us started visiting the ER — more than once — for different reasons, from pneumonia to scarlet fever. Luckily, the hospital had a financial assistance program for people under the poverty line.

We struggled hard to balance everything: work, school, kids. Many times we had to beg our professors to bring at least one baby to the class. When it was time for our practicum, we found it was impossible to arrange, unless we locked our kids in a cage.

And then, another blow struck: I was kicked out of the funding because I didn’t look right in one professor’s eyes. Now, without my part of the income and my wife’s start of her practicum, the food stamp and tax return became our only lifeline. And when I questioned my possibility of pursuing my counseling goal, that professor was straightforward: “Not possible.”

That dream was not totally dead; I could go to another school. But if I needed to go to another school, I wanted a way more ambitious goal: a doctoral degree. I’ve proved many doubters wrong, now I needed to prove her wrong.

It was a very long shot. I’ve always been a cautious and fearful person, but for some reason, when it came to education, I became the biggest gambler.

We borrowed $6,500 from my wife’s friend (who was a saint with a heart of gold) for me to pay my last semester’s tuition to get a “General Psychology” Master’s— a useless degree. Then I carefully selected the programs I’d like to apply to. Long story short, I was accepted by Kansas State University's Social Psychology program.

But here came the problem: my wife was still in her practicum. If we moved now, she wouldn’t have her counseling degree. When I asked K-State if they could keep my position for next year, the answer was no. I’d have to apply again, and no guarantee of acceptance.

When you have a family, any of your life decisions must be for the benefit of the whole family; it’s NOT about you anymore. My family would be more secure if my wife got her degree. Therefore, I gave up my position and stayed home to care for the kids for a year.

A tough year went by. I applied again — and was accepted. But not only that, this time, I got the Teaching Assistant funding — which, again, meant a full tuition waiver and a yearly stipend — but because K-State was a way bigger school, the stipend was twice as much. The wait turned out to be the better outcome.

We moved to Manhattan, Kansas, a small and friendly town full of hope. I knew perfectly I wasn’t equipped to be a Ph.D. student but figured maybe I could fool the whole program even just a semester. A “partial completion of a doctoral degree” still sounded better than a master’s degree.

With our budget, we couldn’t find a place to rent for a family of 5. Finally we got a very small basement; a regular McDonald’s dining space would be at least three times as big. It was depressing to squeeze two adults and three children into that dungeon; we just had to fight to survive.

Then it was my wife’s turn to receive a blow from life: she couldn’t find a job. After 3 months, she fell into depression. She wouldn’t talk to me, wouldn’t engage in any activity, lay on the sofa and cried all night.

This couldn’t go on, I thought; she had to do something. Anything. “How about applying for a Ph.D. program here?” I suggested.

She did, and was accepted to the Family Study program. She also found a Research Assistant job. With both our incomes and the yearly tax return, we paid off our debt to my wife’s saint friend. Then we moved out of that dungeon to a townhouse. Both of us worked 20 hours per week, completed our assignments/research, and raised three kids. I got another Master’s and then a Ph.D. after 7 years; my wife got her Ph.D. one year after me. I sent out sixty applications for tenure-track faculty positions and got three offers — which was considered very lucky. We ended up at a small university in New Mexico. Right now we both are tenured professors.

That is our story.

So as you can see, throughout our time at K-State, we both got our Master's and PhDs without paying a dime — and got paid. Now you might think we lived like homeless people, or even eating grass and wearing rags. Not at all. Yes, we didn’t have furniture except for mattresses and a dining table. Our car was small without AC and with an overheating problem. But we ate normal food, wore normal clothes, managed to travel to other cities/states for entertainment every summer — and still saved some money. We didn’t live like a king, but comfortable enough.

If you’re dreaming about graduate schools, maybe even a doctoral degree, but worry about not being able to afford it, don’t be afraid. If I can do it, chances are SO CAN YOU. I’d like to share with you my knowledge and experience; hopefully they can be helpful to you.

Graduate school applications can be daunting and intimidating, and there’re many factors to consider. In this article, let’s simply focus on the financial part.

First, it’s my philosophy: try your best NOT to get into debt. I know student loans are popular, but before you’ve truly exhausted all the options, I strongly suggest you don’t consider that. When I was applying for Ph.D. programs, my thought was, “If they don’t give me enough money, I simply won’t go.” Getting into debt is like having bubble gum in your hair. True, we borrowed $$ from my wife’s friend, but it was HER who sensed our critical problems and volunteered, and she told us, “Don’t worry about when to pay it back, and no interest.” We managed to pay her off in about two years, but we owed her almost our lives. I highly doubt your loans will be half as forgiving and understanding like my wife’s friend.

Now, if no loans (at least for now), then what options do you have?

Before I tell you the secret, it goes without saying that you need to be a hard-working student (also, it’s a big plus if you have at least some research experience; we’ll talk about it in another article), because that maximizes your chance of getting paid in graduate schools.

The secret: when you apply for graduate schools, you have to find out every detail about their TEACHING/RESEARCH assistantships.

Once you decide on the programs you want to apply to, ask them about the teaching/research assistantships. This is how we got paid for our PhDs and raised a family. Graduate schools, especially bigger research-oriented schools, have more money and therefore more positions to hire graduate students to work. The positions mainly fall into these two categories.

A teaching assistant position requires you to help a professor such as grading, proctoring exams, and other tasks the professor sees fit. Usually, these positions are available in the department you’re applying to, though there might be some positions available in other departments sometimes (it’s rare though, cause people like to hire students within their own departments for the teaching position). The great things about this position are: first, in quite a few cases, it offers a full tuition waiver — you do NOT have to pay a dime. Even if it’s not a full waiver, you should only pay in-state tuition (that’s called a partial waiver), which is way, way cheaper than the full tuition. Second, it has a stipend (but usually only nine months; no job for the summer). Also, you get to see how professors teach and handle a class. Some even let you teach a class. Many schools (like K-State) even have a training program for graduate students to be full-time instructors (which means more extra cash). If you’re interested in teaching, this is a great opportunity. When I got my Ph.D. and started looking for a job, I already had about five years of teaching experience under my belt, which was a big help. Though because this is a position with potential, it might not be available for every student, and only lasts about three to five years. But if this year you didn’t get it, wait for the next year and apply again, you might get lucky.

A research assistant position is usually available everywhere on campus. It’s called “research assistant,” but sometimes it doesn’t involve a lot of research. Basically, whenever a program or a department wants to hire some student workers, they post the position and all students around campus are eligible to apply. The good things about this position are: first, it offers a partial tuition waiver, so you only have to pay in-state tuition. Second, the stipend can sometimes be up to twelve months, more than the teaching assistant. Finally, it’s more available than the teaching assistant position cause you can apply everywhere.

Now, let’s suppose you get either a TA or RA, congrats! Now you have to strive to keep this job. Work hard, kick ass, be helpful. This is not the time to fight with anyone unless you’re at the receiving end of prejudice/harassment. About three years into my TA, another more long-term teaching position was available, and because I was somewhat a popular instructor they thought of me. That was why I could support my family for seven years at K-State. And here I can’t express my sincere appreciation to every professor in the department enough. They’d helped me whenever I needed it.

Indeed, from my story, you can see that we were extremely lucky. For a very long time, we were way, way below the poverty line, and we struggled hard. But at every turn, some good things happened and we prevailed. However, you don’t have to struggle like we did, especially if you don’t have little kids. Plus, chances are English is your native language, you don’t have to worry about fighting to understand what others say, having serious self-doubt about whether you can teach in another language to the point of a panic attack. The first time I taught, it was an assignment from a professor, and his class had 150 students. I rehearsed the whole fifty-minute lecture, every single word I’d say, at least five times. When I got on the stage, my mouth was dry like fire, my heart was pounding in my ears. But then I thought of all the exciting things I wanted to tell them, and I opened my mouth.

Now I’m a tenured professor.

My best wishes to you. Feel free to ask any questions, and I will write more about preparation for graduate school applications every week. Thanks and hope all is well with you.

items.title: Chu News
items.summary: We Bring You the True News I created this blog to share with readers  Scientific findings, Social issues, My personal stories and/or observations, And a bit of my creative fiction writing. Except for the creative fiction, which will be noted, all the writings are based…
items.content_html:
We Bring You the True News

I created this blog to share with readers 

Scientific findings,

Social issues,

My personal stories and/or observations,

And a bit of my creative fiction writing.

Except for the creative fiction, which will be noted, all the writings are based on scientific research and/or facts.  If they involve personal opinions, they will be specified and the readers can be the judge.

The purpose of heavily relying on science is not to say that religion and personal opinions are not important.  Plus, science is created by humans, and humans are not perfect.  However, the main issue is that science is much more reliable than personal beliefs. 

Why?  There are as many beliefs as the number of humans, and they all think they're right.  Just think, "This must be it and I know I'm right" is not going to make it a fact.  It's good to generate a hypothesis, but then one has to investigate and be willing to be proven wrong if that's the case, 

It's literally impossible to always be right, yet many see themselves that way.  The so-called "common sense" or "intuition" is as reliable as a career politician.

Some might question the validity of science and experts.  And indeed, they can make mistakes.  Actually, that is the point of science: To investigate and correct mistakes in order to find out the truth and facts.  That is why science heavily relies on the peer-review process.  

The fact that we enjoy cars, electricity, architecture, MRI machines, computers, artificial intelligence, etc., etc. is all because of science.  Like democracy, it's not perfect, but it's the best tool we have.

Therefore, in Chu News, we believe a basic understanding of science is critical in modern society.  And thus, we share.

items.title: About Me
items.summary: Hi all, My name is Mark Chu.  I'm originally from Taiwan, but now living in the US.  I'm married and have three children. I'm a professor in psychology at a tiny university in a remote Southwest town.  My field, specifically, social psychology, is a scientific…
items.content_html:
Hi all,

My name is Mark Chu.  I'm originally from Taiwan, but now living in the US.  I'm married and have three children.

I'm a professor in psychology at a tiny university in a remote Southwest town.  My field, specifically, social psychology, is a scientific field studying human behavior in different situations.  My research focus is on sexism, but I'm also interested in adult attachment styles.

My interests include basketball, creative writing (fiction or nonfiction), drawing, and music, to name a few.

Thank you for visiting.

items.title: The Edinburgh People I Met Cannot Be Nicer - Part 1 of 3
items.summary: [Chu News — Mark Chu / Travel] Can we talk about the people in Edinburgh being so nice, you can't even begin to fathom what is going on? So this May (2026), my wife and I went to the UK for two weeks. It was…
items.content_html:
[Chu News — Mark Chu / Travel] Can we talk about the people in Edinburgh being so nice, you can't even begin to fathom what is going on?

So this May (2026), my wife and I went to the UK for two weeks.  It was the first time we went abroad to a place other than Taiwan and America.  I chose London and Edinburgh, mostly because our oldest son went there a few years ago and couldn't stop talking about them.  I somewhat thought, "Well, if this trip isn't as good, at least we have someone else to blame."

We were a bit nervous: A place we weren't familiar with, what if we got lost?  Couldn't figure out how to pay whatever fees?  Had no means to pay?  You know, all the greenie, naive tourists' typical worries.

After a ten-hour flight with Virgin Atlantic, in which the on-flight entertainment didn't work at all, and they didn't have a regular phone charger plug-in (I guess European airlines are the same?) so I couldn't use my phone, we arrived.  

And lo and behold, right after getting out of the airport looking for The Tube, we got lost.  After aimlessly walking round and round, in and out of the Tube gates, arguing where the right places were, a couple was kind enough to notice our struggle and led us to the right way.  Not only that, they asked us where we were going and told us how to get there.  So now, Londoners made a good first impression on us.

When we walked on the street, the first things that welcomed us were cars driving on the left side and weather as stable as my mood.  This minute it was sunny, the next minute it started raining.  After a long walk dragging our suitcases, we found our hotel.  

(Looking out of the windows from our hotel stairways)

I think not only in the UK, but also in other European countries, things typically equipped by default in an American hotel room aren't found here.  For example, a mini fridge, or a microwave.  Also, there are no washer and dryer in the hotel.  No free breakfast.  I have to say the customer service was not bad though.  He started a small chat, and agreed to keep our suitcases in a storage room before check-in.

A friend of ours in London described the weather in that particular day, "Going through four seasons in one day."

We were sitting in a small Mediterranean restaurant looking at the storm in awe. The boss was outgoing and friendly, and the food was great.  We loved the lentil soup.  And the best yet was NO TIP required.  In America, the service isn't any better at all, and they demand 20% more.  

The next day, while I was walking and about to turn right, I heard the bell from a bike.  Without thinking, I kept walking, until a high schooler on a bike turning left was about to hit my face.  Right away I realized my mistake: I was walking on the right.  But the high schooler said, "Sorry," and rode away.  I believe I said sorry as well.  It was my fault, not his fault anyway, yet he apologized. 

Small things.  Though up to this point, I was convinced Londoners were just very polite and nice.

Many times while we walked around, we saw dogs without leashes.  But like their owners, they were either friendly or simply minding their own business.  By golly, are London dogs trained by the culture too?  Have gone to manners school?

My wife wanted to go to a park to see birds and took photographs.  This was the main purpose of the trip.  She found St James's Park that seemed to be worth a visit, so off we went.  But we had no idea what kind of place it was and what we were walking into.

My wife heard a lot of birds singing coming from the building on our right and said, "I need to find a way in." I saw all the sharp spikes on the top of the walls and said, "I don't think you can... this looks like a very important building."

There were many people from all over the world taking pictures, and at that moment we realized we had turned into tourists admiring this attraction.  What's funny is I've lived in America for about thirty years, and the first capitol building I've visited outside of Taiwan's government was the UK's.  No, I've never been to White House yet.

I guess he was in his 60s.  It was kind of chilly, but he wore light clothes and was barefoot.  And he was a bird whisperer.  Out of nowhere, he greeted us and started talking about the birds while feeding them.  He was knowledgeable, very sociable, and enthusiastic.  My wife got a few good bird pics. 

But suddenly we heard a scream, a teenage girl crying out loud.  Everyone turned to look at what was going on.  The girl was probably on the spectrum, and her toy or something important to her was dropped into a restricted area.  Her mom was comforting her.  I think they believed that the thing was lost forever.  A guard who was far away on the other side saw what happened, ran into the area, picked the thing up, gave it to the girl, and had a quick chat with them.  Everything was fine now.

I guess we indeed experienced a piece of the UK in the park.  We enjoyed it.

The next day, we visited Chiswick House and Gardens, right next to our hotel.  It was big, with many people jogging, walking dogs, or resting and chatting.  By now, readers should know that pretty much every garden in the UK is beautiful, but here are a few photos presented as evidence.

However, the garden, though pretty, had a smell of dogs' poop lingering everywhere.  Animals have the right to enjoy the place as much as we do, so we don't complain.

The garden has a restaurant, and right next to it is the public bathroom.  The men's room was ok, but my wife said there was neither tissue paper nor soap in the women's.  Bummer.

We have a friend in London.  He and his husband couldn't be nicer.  They brought us places and treated us to all the fancy meals.  They made sure we knew what the UK was like and our trip was worthwhile.

One of the best ways to learn about a culture, of course, is food.  They brought us to a traditional English breakfast place, Ace Cafe London.  The food was great.  Unfortunately, I didn't think to take a picture until halfway done...

We didn't know London had a lot of canals.  Some were like overpasses with boats roaming on them.  We were very amazed.

This is one canal.

 

 

It was in the afternoon, we were down on the street, and we noticed a huge crowd gathering.  They were shouting and singing.  "Football fans," our friend said.  There was a big game coming, and the whole area was packed with people, and I'm sure some (or many) with alcohol in their hands.  Sometimes they became so loud, you'd think a big fight would start any time.  But they were just very enthusiastic.

That's definitely a lovely cultural phenomenon I like to see.

Whether you know about the issues or not, whether you like it or not, I believe we agree that everyone must fully support basic human rights.  Basic.  Human. Rights.

 

The following day, our friends took us to the University of Cambridge.  I always wanted to go there; three of my favorite Brits all graduated from there -- David Attenborough, Hugh Laurie, and John Oliver (by last name alphabetical order).  It must be a great school.  By the way, Happy 100th Birthday, Sir Attenborough!

I didn't know, but the U of Cambridge is in the city of Cambridge--and there is not a "gate," "fence," or "wall" for the campus.  All the colleges, departments, buildings are spread around the city.  So we simply walked around to visit as many places as possible.

 

The next day we visited this vegetarian restaurant.  I vowed never to visit the same restaurant twice so we could try different food, good or bad.  This one was ok--and surprise, it asked for a tip.  I think I could have chosen "no tip;" after all, this is the UK, but I gave a little.  Bad habit.

The last one was arguably the best.  What do you think?

And this concluded our stay in London.  It's a great city.

Next, we head toward another great one, Edinburgh.
